commentBody: Are pesos and euros DINERO because they can both be Spanish words?
Record Friday time. I wish it was because I'm getting smarter, but I don't think that's the case.
JUST SO WE'RE CLEAR, I'm glad that having gotten SURPRISE... for [Things that send people into hiding], "raid" was too short to complete the entry.

Pleasant answers, as I would expect from these two pros.
Too many easy clues, which cannot be on these two pros.

One should not be able to say about a Musa/Goldstein Friday crossword: ITSASNAP.
2 minutes faster than my Monday
Monday easy
Fun solve but definitely a Monday puzzle
32A makes this a great puzzle and is an OMEN that the Giants will do well this season.

Yikes. 
I’m a dummy that takes like 25-35 minutes to do an average Friday.  Today was 12?

The comments are gonna be brutal. 

I read Thursdays comments less than 15 minutes ago. If @Lewis is unhappy with the cluing / editors, then you know we have jumped the shark (shout out Fonzie). 

Editors be like “GO EASY ON ME”
Loved the clue for GLUTENFREE! I have celiac, I guess I go against the grain!
"[Things that send people into hiding]" reads a bit off, these days.
go easy okay? Not okay.

Upper right quadrant took me a little bit to finish, but otherwise pretty easy for a Friday.  I mostly enjoyed it, since there felt like a bit of chew throughout for me.

XWStats is showing "Very Easy" at present.  I wouldn't go that far.

🤷‍♂️

I like today's puzzle, the kind of indirect cluing that is confusing at first but makes perfect sense when you think about it a different way.  GLUTEN FREE in particular.

My only trouble with the puzzle was getting SURPRISE PARTIES, because I originally had SET for 'Gym circuit' instead LAP and STAMP instead of TRAMP.  I figured 'James Wets' was someone I hadn't heard of just like I hadn't heard of HASAN Piker.  Which led me to be trying to fill '____RICE PATTIES' which I thought might be some old timey idiom I'd never heard of.  But figuring out it was PARTIES led me to LAP and the rest fell into place.
“Functions without awareness?” is a particularly good alternative for that spanner - kudos for that one. Pretty breezy but a lot of fun!

Not the first and won’t be the last to say ITSASNAP, and  GOEASYONME, were apropos. If these were the hard Friday clues, what could the easy ones be? Doesn’t get any easier than old McDonald and ABCS for fundamentals? These are top tier constructors (and a very smooth grid), surely we could have uplifted these clues a bit more.
That said, I did like the clues for GLUTENFREE, PUBTRIVIA and ALOU but most of the other misdirects were not too misdirecting.
I will say since I mentioned yesterday that my kids picked up an ALBANIAN RESCUE that it was nice today’s crossword finished the thought.
Nice puzzle..for a Tuesday. PB for me.
Giant Jesus was a great clue and philosophy of a devil's advocate was pretty good. Otherwise this was Monday worthy. Just so we're clear, I don't think boring spanners enhance any puzzle.

"36D. You don’t have to do any good deeds to satisfy this meaning of [Be a hero, say] — the answer is COSPLAY."

IMHO, that one's obscure/tortured to the point that I suspect that was the only combination of letters the constructor could fit in to complete the puzzle, so they had to look up some obscure contraction, COSPLAY = COSTUME PLAY, i.e a "hero" might appear in a costume play. A good later-in-the week/clue is one if the solver doesn't get it other than by the cross-words, but then looks at what they should have gotten, they say, "Oh yeah!", not "What???"

PETA and RESCUES crossing with GAME REFUGE warmed this animal lovers heart. 💜 

And PELE so DINOSAUR Matt can learn about football.
Had  “cow” instead of “sow” and “tramp” instead of “stamp” for a bit, and couldn’t reconcile what kind of “rice patties” would and people into hiding.  Not old enough for ‘Nam either.

What a treat – a Rafa and Rebecca puzzle! 
I love these two constructors so much that I'm not even going to apologize for being on a first name basis with them, like old friends, lol.

These two are the bee's knees and the cat's meow. Put them together and it's magic squared!

Thank you so much!

A bit too easy for a Friday as well as for what these two and Will Shortz et al are capable of with wordplay and misdirects.
To the Games higher-ups: please leave the Crossword be. Please show respect for the gold standard and those who have given of themselves to cement it.

Didn't understand how 'Gukesh Dommaraju, upon becoming world chess champion in 2024' translates to TEEN. Gukesh was already a teenager before he becomes the chess champion and is still a teenager - so where does the 'upon becoming' bit come into the picture? Just seemed like a very awkwardly worded clue.
Sacré bleu, Hastings! Poirot prefers a little more challenge to the little gray cells in a Friday puzzle.

Fairly easy Friday, things dropped quickly into place, with only a few, easily fixed, incorrect entries.

Nicely constructed, but clueing could have been a bit more devious.

Thanks, Rebecca and Rafa!
I don't mean to be harsh, but let's be honest.  It was pleasant enough, but more like a mega-Mini.

It’s fresh, it’s clean, it’s smooth… and yet it also was in *no way, shape, or form a Friday*

JUSTSOWERECLEAR… ITSASNAP

🫤

Today was my first ever clue-free Friday, a personal Everest—until I got here and found out it was more of a scenic overlook.

I’m planting my flag anyway.
This was such fun! Loved the long answers. Good cluing. Just a gem. Thanks, Rafael and Rebecca! Happy Friday, y’all!

I’d like to clarify a previous comment. Two things can be true at once: the fill was delightful and the puzzle was uncharacteristically easy _for its day_.

That’s not being dismissive or pretentious or unappreciative. For those of you completing your first Friday sans lookup, rock on. But this ain’t how they used to make ‘em, in my humble opinion.

Again, Fridays and Saturdays shouldn’t have to shatter your will to puzzle. They should _not_ feel like a large Mini or a Midi, either.
Basically a Tuesday

I found this a proper Friday challenge - my solve time was right on the money for this time of the week. I see many others found thought grid way too easy, so maybe my being Polish influenced my solving experience even more than usual, but for once I'm glad about it! 

I enjoyed the misdirection of many of the clues. However, I thought the trivia-heavy NE would defeat me: I had no idea what Zillow or a kachina might be, any mention of American legislation *for short* strikes terror into me, and I feared I would not be able to get the name of today's particular Jesus. But I decided to persevere. You probably know the answers, I thought to myself, and sure enough, with a few crosses I remembered ACA, ALOU (thanks, Steve L!), and HOPI. I admit I Googled what Zillow is to get an idea of what the answer to its clue might be, but that ended up being my only bit of outside help.

My wife and I visited Denmark in 2024 and yet I already managed to forget they don't use the Euro! I suppose it's because it's a completely cashless society - all I did was wave my phone around terminals, and I never handled a single Danish krona. It's not like I use cash in Poland either, I guess, not outside the farmer's market anyway, so maybe I should not be that surprised...
Relatively easy apart from ALOU. I hate name clues where you're SOL if you don't know a random person.

So it’s themeless and thus will land on Friday or a Saturday. 

Not much trivia to artificially make it hard.  

So in order to make it a Friday we’re left with the cluing being fiendish. Well it wasn’t. It was benign. 

A nice set of answers that merited better.

Spelling Bee: Today’s pangram can’t possibly be a coincidence—today is the day that every fourth-year medical student across the country finds out where they will start residency on July 1. No spoilers here, but if this was a little Easter egg, then thank you! A big day in many lives.
Calling an [Animal sanctuary] a GAME REFUGE is a little like calling a state park a meat farm.  It sends mixed messages.  Am I going there to relax, or am I on the menu?

It was a fun puzzle! Came together very easily except for that NE corner. I struggled with that MESAS ACA ALOU stack. Had to look up what Zillow was and then put in HOMErates which didn't help much. Also was stuck on OPERAhat for the longest time and couldn't figure out what the extra letter was lol. Finally ended up needing a look up (ACA) to finish. Still got done in under my average Friday time though!

So this would have been difficult for me - but I ruthlessly looked up the names (HASAN, ALOU, AGGIE, OLSEN) and US-only words (Zillow, ACA, MAINE, HOPI) and trivia (NAENAE) and after that it wasn't so difficult. 
PELE I knew, and SCOT and WATT (there's a university in Edinburgh called Herriot-Watt).
I can see why some solvers would say it was too easy.

Cute Wednesday-level puzzle. This took me 1/3 of my average Friday time and earned me a Friday personal best. I enjoyed the clueing, but not the fact that it took me about as long to do the puzzle as to read the article afterwards.

XW Stats called this “Very Easy,” with 95% of solvers playing faster than their average Friday time, and almost 80% solving it in over 20% less than their Friday average. Stats don’t lie. I used to look forward to the challenging Friday and Saturday puzzles, but lately I feel annoyed more often than delighted. 

The other NYT games (esp Connections) have gotten way too easy lately also.

A calm 14 min, my Friday record! Love the clues but they did feel more Tuesday, Wednesday-ish. 

No problem with the puzzle itself, these are actually two of my favourite constructors! Just that I look forward to a challenge on Fri and Sat.
Got me thinking Hemingway, with the OMEN and the SEE.

Fun puzzle, thank you. 

Am I wrong to think that the chess clue is incorrect? "Upon becoming the champion" would mean that as a result of his win, he then became a teen. But he was a teen first.... do I have my phrasing wrong?
That was fun to solve. I was brought back to earth due to my misspelling SURPRICE. COW wasn’t it.

Let's assume the finance department is calling the shots on clue composition -- is it fair to say the target audience is not even bothering with the puzzle in the first place?

Meanwhile, time to see what's cooking over at The Atlantic.
So who exactly at NYT is giving this rush order? Just so we’re clear.

Random thoughts:
•	What a lovely addition to the crossword answer oeuvre – JUST SO WE’RE CLEAR.  It’s not only its first appearance in the Times puzzle, but in all the major outlets.
•	Two echoes to yesterday’s puzzle:  NAE NAE after yesterday’s neighboring NAY NEE, and ASSET, which backwards is yesterday’s TESSA.
•	I liked the fauna sub-theme – GAME REFUGE, SOW, MOO, RESCUES, EWE, and DINOSAUR.
•	There was a sports related undercurrent as well: Soccer (PELE), baseball (ALOU), swimming (LAP), auto racing (TIRE), and football (IT’S A SNAP).
•	[Giant Jesus] immediately triggered the image of one in the Macy’s Thanksgiving Day parade.
•	PuzzPair© -- DINOSAUR / OLD ME.
•	NAE NAE and the moo-moo clue got me seeing SEE as “si-si”, and EYE as “aye-aye”.

Congratulations on your fourth collab in the Times, R&R, and thank you.  I had great fun with this!

Could someone please explain 56 down MOS for "approaches"?

Looked for a comment here - am I alone in my puzzlement?

Thank you.

“Monday easy”
“Like a midi”

Really?

I may not be the most experienced solver here, but I’m not an idiot, either. And I found this plenty challenging. 

I’m starting to think I don’t belong here.
This was a Friday.
Another Friday cakewalk. C’mon NYT, last Saturday (or was it the Saturday before?) showed you had a little more in the bag than that.

This was very easy for a Friday and I solved it in 5:41 for a new PB. JUST SO WE'RE CLEAR I don't think it was a bad puzzle and it still AMUSEd me, but I'd prefer a bit more of a challenge at the end of the week.
Pretty easy for a Friday. 🙃

@Sam Corbin

I always like it when the Games staff or the constructors are scanning the Comments and adding their thoughts.
However, I'd like to see more than just words of encouragement.
As I trust you can tell, the overwhelming question inside the Commentariat is, "Are the editors/constructors getting instructions from 'higher-ups' to ease up on the level of difficulty of the puzzles?"

The silence of the Games staff is deafening!

Functions without awareness? It me!

That would have been a great clue. In fact I would have put it in Lewis's top clues of the week if I still ran it.

All those clues Rebecca pitched for one entry show how hard the constructors work. Thanks, constructors! Fun puzzle.

Can't help wondering if Heart would have still had a hit song if the lyrics had been:

"Let me go easy on you
Easy on you
Let me go easy, easy on you, oh"

A wicked good puzzle.
I had no idea there were so many islands off the coast of Maine.
Felt like a themeless Tuesday puzzle today. Sad.

Seeing "Giant Jesus" in the puzzle reminded me of an old joke. 

Apparently Jesús, Felipe, and Matty had a fourth brother who made it into the majors.  But Boog changed his last name to Powell because he didn't want to be known as...

Boog Alou.
Fun puzzle. Good for Wednesday.

Nice puzzle, but a typical Friday workout for me. Actually a bit surprised that so many found it unusually easy. No big deal - that's just me. My usual puzzle finds today. First one just a rather amazing feat of construction - A Friday from December 13, 2002 by Bob Peoples. Three triple stacks of grid-spanning 15 letter answers... across the top, middle and bottom. And among those were five answers making their one and only appearance, but they were all familiar phrases. Just a bit surprised by that. Some of those one-time appearances: CARDIGANSWEATER SATELLITERADIOS EMERALDEARRINGS STUDIOAUDIENCES TARGETPRACTICES Here's that link: https://www.xwordinfo.com/Crossword?date=12/13/2002&g=65&d=A I'll put my other puzzle find in a reply. ....
COSPLAY? Never heard of it. Next clue, "Get it?" Now I do.
Fun puzzle overall, but the clue for DINERO ("pesos or euros") irked me--dinero is a Spanish word, so shouldn't the clue have been in Spanish?
Nicely crafted puzzle - but not for a Friday. Thank God for the Archives.
Possibly the easiest Friday puzzle I've ever played. One highlight: the clueing for 17A, “Going against the grain?” That one gave me a chuckle.
I just came here to say that "Giant Jesus" had me rolling. 🤣🤣🤣🤣

I was considering all sorts of dire things that send people into hiding, as I moved along through my first pass.
Yaay...SURPRISEPARTIES!

Most horses who are not "in the wild" are SHOD.  There are numerous reasons for keeping hooves protected; horseshoes can even be corrective.  

BIRTHDAY PARTIES fit in there nicely.....Hide and Go Seek isn't a bad party game.  

ESCOOTERS? The way people charge around in their giant vehicles?  No thanks.

I can't quite get my head around the "new word" COSPLAY.  Wasn't "Pretend" good enough?  

And isn't it about time to GO EASY ON Hillary Clinton?  We have villains enough who merit investigation and opprobrium, who are without the education, accomplishments, and record of service that she has to her credit.  It would be nice to have a few people in office who were as qualified and hard-working, IMHO.

I don't understand the clue "Gukesh Dommaraju, upon becoming world chess champion in 2024" at 61A.  If the clue had been written "Like Gukesh Dommaraju, world chess champion in 2024", the answer "teen" would make sense.  However, the clue as it is written, to me, seems to suggest Gukesh Dommaraju became a teen after (during?) the 2024 world chess championship.  Was this individual age 12 at the beginning of the tournament/match and became age 13 after winning, or only became a teen because they won the championship?
I thought the clue for GLUTENFREE was fantastic!
I need a spoiler warning if they're going to give away the answers to the long clues!
Waaaay too easy for a Friday but I can't complain too much. This puzzle and its fun clues were a thing of beauty.

So there aren't FUR PRICE PARTIES where one can learn about a great deal on hides? Not with 4D in the same puzzle?

Had to go look up Mr Piker but now I'll check him out.
Enjoyable puzzle but more like a Wednesday in terms of difficulty. The ‘Giant Jesus’ gave me a chuckle when I figured it out; always fun to see a common answer clued in a creative way.
Other than working the back catalog, is there anywhere one can find actual Friday-worthy puzzles these days? I’m happy to breeze through a Monday, but I really crave my late-week challenge.
Totally delightful.
I’m re-enjoying the Outlander series, so SCOT was a gimme. Nice when guilty pleasures contribute to each other.

Well… there goes the nice streak we had for challenging Fridays and Saturdays. I echo with Barry said: again, this isn’t on the constructors. NYT has to keep them easy so that people keep subscribing.
The NYT should add a Wednesday themeless, for beautifully constructed, totally enjoyable puzzles, like today's, but that are easier than what people expect for a Friday.
[British engineer James] was actually Scottish!
An OPERA Cape is dramatic, an OPERA COAT is functional. (I've never been to the opera, actually, but I can picture Groucho wearing one, dramatically.)

Got a new PB which was equal to my average Tuesday solve time.  

And that included scratching my head for a few secs when I had 
__URPRIcEPAtTIES [cOW instead of SOW, stAMP instead of TRAMP]

For about a half sec, I thought “do burps from rice patties” send people into hiding?

Not complaining about the difficulty - I needed something soft after restless nights recently.

Today's poem made from words found in today’s puzzle

a/ rush in heavily rush
unceremoniously
in to the scenery
d/ of eternity
be hero to
the story
to things
known
be thirst
for the hiding things
a/ just go
asap
I guess Friday is the new Tuesday

JUST SO WE'RE CLEAR:  This puzzle HASAN identity problem. It did not AMUSE me.  Have I once asked the NYT to GO EASY ON ME?  I HOPI never again see a Friday about which I can truly say: IT'S A SNAP.  I think that on Fridays we're entitled to AFFAIR challenge.  Not to the equivalent of doing one's ABCS in kindergarten.  I would rather have a puzzle that occasionally makes me feel I'M LOST than one that PITIES me.  

NAE NAE, I protest.  NAE NAE,   I want my Fridays back.

Hasanabi heads rise! 
Nice puzzle but as others have said, quite easy for a Friday.
I enjoyed Sam's SNL video link, but I had to look twice at the original air date: just 5 years ago.  Were real estate listings really that cheap in 2021?  That WOULD be sexy.
Really liked this one. It was like I went back in time (I've been doing the NYT puzzle since the early 90's). A good Friday entry.
The seat heaters in our car keep our buns warm.

I was really enjoying the clueing and having fun with this (and omg why do so many people care that it wasn’t hard enough for a Friday?) but I really got hung up on the paganism clue. My husband has been calling me jokingly a pagan all these years because I wasn’t baptized and Wiccan and other pagan beliefs often center on natural cycles, not any kind of devil worship. I don’t usually get offended on here but I was today.
This might be the first Friday I've finished without looking at the blog! And way under my usual time, too.

Aw, c'mon.  My criticism wasn't any worse than anyone else's, though, I hope, maybe a little more inventive?  No bad words of any kind, I'm quite sure of that.  What has disturbed the bots THIS  time?  Print the darn thing, will ya.  I've tried to post it twice.

Sadly, I'm losing that exciting tinge of nervousness when clicking on a Thursaday/Friday/Saturday puzzle.
I enjoyed this one, plenty of nice entries and clues etc, but finishing in 15min is disappointing for a Friday. Almost down at my Monday average time, and a few minutes below my Tuesday average.
Hopefully tomorrow will be a real b*stard.

I enjoyed today's clue, but with my best Fact Boy hat on, as one who cooks regularly for someone with Celiac I note that wheat, barley and rye must be avoided, not all grains. 


3 results for GLUTENFREE from Modern Era puzzles:
Fri Mar 20, 2026	17A	
Going against the grain?	
Rafael Musa and Rebecca Goldstein	
Sun Apr 25, 2021	59A	
Like bread made from almond flour	
Jeremy Newton	
Sat Jan 25, 2014	32D	
Like some diets that avoid pasta	
James Mulhern

I don't envy whoever's job it is to determine which puzzles run on which days. You have to figure out if more people are going to complain that it's too hard, or more people are going to complain that it's too easy. Because either way, people are going to complain, apparently. I just like solving puzzles.

Adding to the chorus of “this is too easy” 

My Friday, Saturday, and Sunday times recently have looked more like Monday, Tuesday, and Wednesday times. 

Please give us something more deserving of our subscription fees, editors!
As others have said, I did this in about my average Tuesday time, but the fill was delightful. I enjoyed the little cluster of PETA, GAMEREFUGE, and RESCUES. Giant Jesus was diabolically clever.
When I do the Sudoko on NYTimes Games, I have the option for Easy, Medium or Hard. If crosswords were handled in this way, maybe there wouldn't be so many upset people on both sides of the fence.
Easiest Friday puzzle ever! Fun anyway. Giant Haysoos--lol, finally got that one!

Is anyone else finding that the midi puzzle is nearly impossible to get to most days? Certain other puzzles link to it at the bottom menu of the page, but then often clicking it just brings up a page that says the page doesn't exist... Others don't list it at all. And this seems to vary by the day. The menu bar sometimes lists it, but not all the time. I find myself spending way too much time searching for it every day - it's like chasing down a ghost! Today I couldn't nail it down until I went to Twitter to see if anyone else has commented on this (apparently no one has?) and then clicked a link to it from serveral days ago. Feel like I'm going crazy, is this just some weird glitch in my browser somehow? I really like the midi, hoping this is just weird website glitching that clears up soon!

Maybe a touch soft for a Friday, but still a rare puzzle these days not overstuffed with proper nouns and obscura. The misleading clues were playful rather than infuriating. More of this please, really enjoyable!

Living in Minnesota these last several months, I had a much darker initial answer for the reason why people might go into hiding. The lovely woman who cleans our home has only just resumed business last week, since about Christmas. So glad to see her. She definitely doesn't yet feel completely safe, but she needs to make a living. 

Anyhow, fun puzzle... Some rather amusing clues. I really liked reading the alternate cluing in the constructor notes for surprise party. 

Though I shivered a bit seeing the two constructor names, thinking it was going to be a really tough one for me, and I was ready for it! However, I solved it over 60% faster than my Friday average and it's my new personal best, by far. 

I've only read a handful of comments, but I can imagine there's a lot of unhappiness... I could rarely be unhappy about a puzzle that offers me any humor and distraction, but if I solved it that quickly, I can only imagine how fast the more experienced solvers solved it. 

That's okay, I'll just do more of my job! Har! 

Cheers to the weekend!! May we somehow all be happy with our crosswording experiences tomorrow.
Insultingly easy. What is the editorial team thinking running this on a Friday? Finished in 5+ minutes, only because I was half paying attention to a March Madness game while solving.

OK, I’m emerging from lurking mode (which has resulted from simply being busy) to add my voice to the ITS A SNAP chorus. Yes, I’m always wishing for themeless Wednesdays—just not on Friday, please. I’ve become a pretty good solver…but not that good.

(The puzzle did not disappoint in other ways; would’ve been a delight on Wednesday .)

Well, this was delightful and fun, and I know folks are complaining it was too easy to run on a Friday, but I was not insulted or annoyed or anything remotely close to that. I do crosswords for the fun of it, and to help assure me that I haven't quite lost my marbles yet, even though I'm old and sometimes can't remember names. I really enjoy reading other solvers's reactions to the puzzle, but I could do without the griping and name-calling, and ----well, enough of that. I guess I'm griping too, so I'll go back out and finish my daily 5 miles in this beautiful spring weather. Then I'll look at a few photos of my adorable and sweet grandchildren. They always makes me smile. :-)
I think James Watt would have taken exception to being called "British," as would most people from Scotland.
"Giant Jesus" being Alou is wild.  They spelled Jesus wrong.  It has am accent mark over the u.  Possibly the dirtiest funniest clue ever.

I for one, would like to say thank you for including the occasional easy friday puzzle. even though i’m in the minority, I was really excited to finally get to a 5 day steak after over 4 years of daily solving!

Surprised to see that this one was a new Friday PR for me because the solve did not feel easy! Got JUSTSOWERECLEAR filled in one letter off and could not make sense of it.  If I'd known I was solving so fast I might have sat with it a little longer before opening Wordplay to check but oh well. Happy Friday! Hoping this weekend will make my first week long streak.
Thank you Rafael for the little brazilian easter eggs! Made my solve a lot more fun.
Oh my, I was sure “desirable feature” was AB SET! Even if it doesn’t perfectly make sense. In reality it was a much more straightforward answer.
Is it me or are fridays getting easier. I have been playing for a year so maybe it’s me?
Wow, another easy Friday. Fun fill.

@Dan

I'd say it's because they're both currency in Spanish-speaking countries.  And maybe what you said, too.
@Dan I would have expected the clue to be in Spanish, i.e., "Pesos o Euros."

Michael,
The entire clue need not be in a foreign language to signal that the answer will be in that foreign language.
@jam Same and it's kind of a bummer!!

@Steve L

"Attacks" wasn't…

@Barry Ancona
As Rebecca herself told us by providing the list of her rejected clues for SURPRISE PARTIES.
@Tuesday, I’d say.

@DIVAS IVLIVS


As one who grew up in the ALOU brothers era, I loved the clue Giant Jesus.

@DIVAS IVLIVS

I got the point that it had to be a ballplayer but had no idea in the world what the name might be.

@Steve L
I know this is JESUS's clue but will always cherish Felipe from his years managing with the late and much lamented Montreal Expos. He had the remarkable privilege of managing his son, the excellent outfielder Moise Alou who later played for the Chicago Cubs.

@B
The three ALOU brothers, baseball players in the 1960's, were Felipe, Mateo (Matty), and Jesus. Felipe's son Mateo played beginning in 1990, but the brothers are more common here.

@Weak

Yes, please let's go easy on them! I'll explain:

I have an inkling that this is all being as difficult on the editors as it is on those of us who expect the crunchiness. 
We all *know* what the constructors and editors are capable of when it comes to wordplay and misdirects.

And that is why something tells me that they're being made to answer to a higher power that is demanding an accessibility based on the lowest common denominator. 
In fact, I wouldn't doubt it if it's driving them up the wall as much as it is us. 

I really do think the editors' hands are tied.
Whoever is calling the shots on profitability is who's to suffer our complaints, and I'm willing to bet it's not Will Shortz nor any editors on his team!

[Exiting soapbox]

@sotto voce

You're probably right...sigh...

@Chris
Yeah. 
LAP, set, rep  

I had reP. At first. As the crosses were a foreign word and some British dude. Who I will some had something to do with power.  

Also. You wrote tramp and stamp. That made me giggle. If you know, you know. IYKYK.

@sotto voce

Well certainly, it’s not like the editors don’t have the ‘talent’ to create diabolical clues.  I think we can agree on that. 

But who they are answering to?  Who really knows.  It might be Will trying to have an era of ‘easy’ crosswords. But you are right. It might be the higher ups.  

For me personally. I’m not the guy that can solve every crossword. So I need the hard ones to help me build my skillz. So I can be that guy.  But, as you know, archives from 15+ years ago humble you quick. Wednesdays are like todays super Saturdays
@Weak same here, takes, me 25 to 35 usually,  I was done in 11:35? A Year ago it was taking 45 to 60. I can't even do a Monday in 11 minutes.

@sotto voce.   Yes, the lowest common denominator.  The uneducated.  The hoi polloi.  The people who haven’t spent the last twenty years of their lives doing crossword puzzles.  Heaven forbid they might be able to solve a puzzle.  Every puzzle should be so difficult that only the top 1% should ever be able to solve them !!

@Jeff
Homie.  You think NYT is the only place to get access crossword puzzles? 
There is a standard that people expect out of the gray lady, crosswords and more importantly, tal real news. 

Perhaps we should lower the standards for military training so more people can join navy seals.  

How about lowering the the basketball rim to 8 feet so more of the “hoi pilloi” can dunk.  That 10 foot rim be keeping 99% of us from dunking. Unacceptable!

You honestly think people here don’t want others to enjoy solving xwords?  Like we’re gate keeping?  Come on bro.

userLocation: Spring Hill, just south of Armageddon
commentBody:
@sotto voce

Then let 'em resign and testify before Congress.

userLocation: Spring Hill, just south of Armageddon
commentBody:
@Weak

Settle down, Beavis.

@Jeff
That is not the tenor of the complaints here. It is that, instead of a spectrum of puzzles  from simple (Mon, Tue) to brain-cracking (Fri, Sat), there has been a retreat to the middle, possibly skewing to the simple. The result has been the disaffection of many solvers of long-standing.

@Jeff, sotto voce’s comment was not meant to be an insult to the “hoi polloi” but to the NYT. The lowest common denominator meaning the easiest puzzles of the week. Those less experienced have five days of less difficult puzzles. All we ask for is two challenging days a week: Friday and Saturday. Even the usual Thursday trickery—not too difficult but requiring lateral thinking—has been absent lately.
@sotto voce -- Oh, excellent point!

@Barry Ancona

@Heidi

I had a similar experience. High five?

@Heidi Well I, for one, hope you stick around. You DO belong, just as everyone who struggles to solve something that others found easy, and vice versa. What my colleague Deb Amlen once said of the column, which holds true to this day: It's a party, and everyone's invited.
@Sam Corbin Perhaps you could speak directly to some commenters on here who daily see it as their job to make people feel like they are not wanted here.

@Heidi

None of the “too easy!” commentary is directed at you, or any other solvers who are finding the current puzzles plenty challenging. But to be perfectly honest, if you are in that boat, you probably *shouldn’t* be able to solve the average Friday/Saturday puzzles without assistance.

And that’s totally fine! As I see it, the day-of-week difficulty scheme is largely intended to strike a compromise that accommodates solvers with a wide range of abilities. The upshot being: for more experienced solvers, the first couple days of the week are usually cakewalks, and for less experienced solvers, the puzzles later in the week are often unapproachable.

In other words, beginners and advanced solvers can “belong here” simultaneously (“here” being the puzzles themselves, the Wordplay comments section being a whole other beast).

Recently, the editors seem to have prioritized making late-week puzzles approachable for less experienced solvers…with the result that more advanced solvers can often go weeks at a time without getting a puzzle that presents them with a real challenge. And that’s what the complaints are about.

NEWDELHI "Base of a fragrant tree?" BEIRUT And the 'reveal': "Pun crimes committed by the answers to the six starred clues?" @Wendy my granddaughter loves to COSPLAY. I get so many pop culture references thanks to her!

@Barry Ancona @ Amy, Now that's what I'm looking for in this forum!  A bit of pleasant agreement, a bit of sass and education.  I learn something new almost every puzzle.

@Fleur Anne 1000 percent. I'm working my way backwards in the archives (Thursday through Saturday puzzles) and I've reached mid-2016. These puzzles are on a whole 'nother level.

@momonjava I do archive puzzles just because I did not have a digital subscription before the mid-oughties. But the ones from the 1990s are difficult because they make reference to people and events from too long ago, including much I never heard of despite living through decades before the 1990s. Author:

@Mean Old Lady granddaughter and I endlessly played pretend until she was about 10 or 11. We did it when she lived with me, and then via facetime when they moved away. Such great memories - literally hours on the phone together. Now she's 16, so it's cosplay. ❤️💕❤️

@Lacey K
they're not considered spoilers. You can't discuss the puzzle without talking about the answers--both in the column and in the comments. If you're reading the column it's assumed that either you've completed the puzzle, or you've come for some hints.
I am cynical enough to believe this.

@Jake G 

Or, to put it another way, the Times needs to recalibrate the difficulty of the puzzles in order to meet the ability of a larger share of their solvers.  As more people try the puzzle each day, those people who have been solving it for years and like more challenging puzzles represent a smaller percentage of their clientele.

Not quite, marigold. There have been new solvers showing up here for decades, willing to take the challenge and not expecting to solve every puzzle from day one. The issues now are (1) more people seem to expect instant gratification, and (2) readers of The New York Times are a smaller percentage of the new solvers, so the literacy of the new solvers and their interest in information and culture provided by The Times is presumed to be -- and quite likely is -- lower.

@Barry Ancona

I think you are more on the money. I think new solvers used to be more patient, accepting that they may only be able to do the early week initially and then build up to the later ones. Somehow that’s not in the psyche anymore for many younger people. Not only that but maybe people also used to accept that a difficult puzzle could take an hour or longer, and that you might have to let it sit for a while and digest. Now they give up if they can’t solve it immediately.

SP, New type of solver, not necessarily younger solver, IMO. I see complaints about *words* not being *fair* coming from commenters of seemingly all ages. Go back ten years and read the comments on the blog. One or two people may report not knowing a word; nobody calls it a bad puzzle. The trick may be tricky, but nobody says it's bad. Also, no times or streaks! https://archive.nytimes.com/wordplay.blogs.nytimes.com/2016/03/16/dont-cry-for-me/

@Avalon V

@Francis 

While I take your point and completely agree, you'll be happy to know she makes far more than $5 an hour cleaning houses. And she's worth every pen...  nickel!  ☺️ 

It sounds weird and also so privileged of me to be someone who has someone who cleans her home. I'd have never seen that coming growing up. But it's been a lifesaver to hire her this last year when I've been so physically challenged. 

My husband does a ton and never balks at housework or anything, but he does so much already and can't do every single thing.  

She's been a blessing, and I'm blessed to be able afford her at this stage in my life.

@HeathieJ

I offered to my wife to do half of the housework if she would do the other half--this was about 25 years ago--and she said I could do it all if I wanted, but we (who both worked and were raising kids at the time) would be hiring a house cleaner every other week if I didn't.

Our current cleaning lady gets $140 for about 4 hours' work.  I do not ask, or care, whether she is a citizen or if not, what her status is.  

It's mutually beneficial.  We get our house cleaned every two weeks, and she (augmenting her husband's earnings) can take care of her family.

@Steve L 

I feel the same way. It's definitely mutually beneficial. I'm just so grateful that we now have the resources to afford it. There were many years, where I wouldn't have been able to.

And I also do not care what her status is...  I only know that she had to be in hiding for 3 months while we were under the ICE surge attack. That really doesn't mean anything, though. Everyone with darker skin or an accent, regardless of status, has been at greatly increased risk, as I know you know. 

Also, my mother-in-law was only able to come to this country (from Honduras) and eventually bring her children with her because she worked as a housekeeper for decades. She worked hard and was able to bring her kids here so I could meet one of them and marry him. I mean that might not have been her long game, but it worked out for me. 😏
